narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid upper-middle-class family background, you've put significant effort into your law school journey, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40—which is actually stronger than the typical applicant's—and your plan to attend full-time, just like the vast majority of students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated your probability of failure at 53.4%, tipping the scales just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection. This prediction was driven primarily by your lower placement in your school's grade deciles for both Year 1 and Year 3, meaning your high school or early college rankings were in the bottom tenth compared to the average student's mid-level standing. These academic decile rankings stood out as the strongest factors pushing the model toward predicting bar failure for you specifically. On a more positive note, your undergraduate GPA had the most substantial counterbalancing influence, pulling the prediction back toward success more than any other factor, thanks to it exceeding the typical 3.21 average. Your family income bracket also helped in this direction, offering a supportive edge over lower-income peers. Your LSAT score of 26, however, nudged things slightly toward the failure prediction, as it falls well below the dataset average of around 37. While your full-time status provided a minor positive boost, it wasn't enough to outweigh the top concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, you stand out with better-than-average undergrad grades and family support, but your weaker decile rankings and LSAT held you back from matching their profiles. This isn't a judgment on your potential—it's just how these key metrics aligned for the model's bar exam forecast. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to the average, strengthening your early academic record through additional coursework if possible, and highlighting any extenuating circumstances in a revised application or appeal. You're clearly capable, and with targeted improvements, you can shift this prediction in your favor for future opportunities. Keep pushing—we're rooting for you.
